text:
{
  "title": "Ordinal Response Process",
  "group_name": "ordinal_response_tree_decomposition",
  "family": "survey_response_process",
  "summary": "Decompose ordered self-reported lifestyle categories into midpoint selection and endpoint direction signals, treating at-risk status as potentially tied to neutral or moderate response behavior rather than only to clearly protective or adverse answers.",
  "depends_on": [],
  "strategy": "Use only stress_level, sleep_quality, physical_activity_level, and smoking_alcohol. Define ordered triads as protective/midpoint/adverse: stress_level low/medium/high, sleep_quality good/average/poor, physical_activity_level active/moderate/sedentary, and smoking_alcohol no/occasional/yes. For each column create midpoint_flag = 1 for the midpoint label, 0 for either endpoint, and NaN if missing; create endpoint_risk_direction = 1 for the adverse endpoint, -1 for the protective endpoint, 0 for the midpoint, and NaN if missing. Aggregate across nonmissing ordered items into observed_ordered_count, midpoint_fraction, endpoint_fraction, adverse_endpoint_fraction, protective_endpoint_fraction, and net_endpoint_risk = (adverse_endpoint_count - protective_endpoint_count) / max(endpoint_count, 1). If endpoint_count is 0 set net_endpoint_risk to 0; if observed_ordered_count is 0 set fractions to NaN and counts to 0. Leave diet_type and gender unused because they lack a consistent health-intensity order.",
  "expected_signal": "The at-risk class may be concentrated in middle-category and mixed endpoint patterns, while fit and unhealthy cases may more often show consistent protective or adverse endpoints, so explicitly separating midpoint response behavior from health-direction response behavior may improve balanced accuracy; this is motivated by response-style literature on midpoint and extreme responses in ordinal survey items (https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00131644211020103) and student health-behavior research finding healthier, moderate, and unhealthy lifestyle classes (https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/13/2/425).",
  "risk": "This may be partly redundant with raw categorical values and prior self-report polarity ideas, and the ordinal assumptions for occasional substance use and moderate activity are coarse; if the data generator treats these labels as nominal rather than ordered, or if missingness already captures most of the signal, the added contrasts may contribute little."
}
